9.3.2 Methods for Calculating Credit VaR:

Vasicek’s Model:

Utilizes a one-factor Gaussian copula model.
Focuses on time to default for companies in a portfolio.
Commonly used for regulatory capital determination.

Credit Risk Plus:

Proposed by Credit Suisse in 1997.
Similar to insurance industry approaches.
Calculates default losses based on probabilities of individual company defaults.
Incorporates uncertainty in default rates.

CreditMetrics:

Proposed by JPMorgan in 1997.
Uses a Gaussian copula model with a ratings transition matrix.
Considers losses from both defaults and credit rating downgrades.

CreditMetrics vs. Credit Risk Plus:

Should predict the same long-term loss distribution if based on identical assumptions.
Differences arise in the timing, not the magnitude, of predicted losses.
Example Scenario: Loan downgraded from A to BBB in Year 1, BBB to B in Year 2, and defaults in Year 3.
Credit Risk Plus Approach: Assumes no losses in Year 1 and Year 2; calculates the loss only in Year 3.
CreditMetrics Approach: Calculates separate revaluation losses in each of the three years.
The total losses calculated using the CreditMetrics approach should theoretically equal those calculated using the Credit Risk Plus approach.




9.3.3 Credit Spread Risk

Value of credit-sensitive products highly dependent on credit spreads.
VaR or ES calculated considering potential credit spread changes.
Historical Simulation Approach:

Collect last 500 days (or other period) of credit spread changes.
Limitations: Assumes no probability of default, may lack quality data.

Alternative: CreditMetrics Approach:

Utilizes 10-day rating transitions matrix and Monte Carlo simulation.
Samples from matrix and credit spread probability distribution.

title: 9  Credit Risk
text:
9.4 Practice Questions and Problems

9.4.1 Estimating Default Probabilities

What is a rating?
What rating agencies exist and how many categories do they use for investment-grade bonds?
What are the disadvantages of ratings with respect to credit risk for financial institutions?
What are alternative methods for estimating credit risk?
What is meant by a hazard rate?
Calculate the average hazard rate for a B-rated company during the first year from the data in Table in the text.
Calculate the average hazard rate for a BB-rated company during the third year from the data in Table in the text.
A credit default swap requires a semiannual payment at the rate of 60 basis points per year. The principal is $300 million and the credit default swap is settled in cash. A default occurs after four years and two months, and the calculation agent estimates that the price of the cheapest deliverable bond is 40% of its face value shortly after the default. List the cash flows and their timing for the seller of the credit default swap.
Explain the two ways a credit default swap can be settled.
Explain the difference between the default probabilities calculated from historical default experience and those calculated from credit spreads.
The spread between the yield on a three-year corporate bond and the yield on a similar risk-free bond is 50 basis points. The recovery rate is 30%. Estimate the implied average hazard rate per year over the three-year period.
The spread between the yield on a five-year bond issued by a company and the yield on a similar risk-free bond is 80 basis points. Assuming a recovery rate of 40%, estimate the implied average hazard rate per year over the five-year period. If the spread is 70 basis points for a three-year bond, what do your results indicate about the implied average hazard rate in years 4 and 5?
How are recovery rates usually defined?
“The position of a buyer of a credit default swap is similar to the position of someone who is long a risk-free bond and short a corporate bond.” Explain this statement.
Why is there a potential asymmetric information problem in credit default swaps?



9.4.2 xVAs

Explain why a new transaction by a bank with a counterparty can have the effect of increasing or reducing the bank’s credit exposure to the counterparty.
A company offers to post its own equity as collateral. How would you respond?
Can the existence of default triggers increase default risk? Explain your answer.
Give two examples of when (a) wrong-way risk and (b) right-way risk can be expected to be observed.
Explain the term “cure period.”
“Netting means that CVA cannot be calculated on a transaction-by-transaction basis.” Explain this statement.
“DVA can improve the bottom line when a bank is experiencing financial difficulties.” Explain why this is so.
What part of CVA risk is considered a component of market risk by regulators?



9.4.3 Credit Value at Risk

Explain the difference between Vasicek’s model, the Credit Risk Plus model, and CreditMetrics as far as the following are concerned: (a) when a credit loss is recognized and (b) the way in which default correlation is modeled.
Explain what is meant by the constant level of risk assumption.
Use the transition matrix from the text to calculate a transition matrix for three years. What is the probability of a company rated AAA being AAA at the end of two years? What is the probability of it moving to AA?
How can historical simulation be used to calculate a one-day 99% VaR for the credit risk of bonds in the trading book? What are the disadvantages?

title: 10  Operational Risk, Liquidity Risk, and Model Risk
text: 10.1 Operational Risk
crumbs: 10  Operational Risk, Liquidity Risk, and Model Risk

title: 10  Operational Risk, Liquidity Risk, and Model Risk
text:
Regulators introduced operational risk capital requirements in 1999.
Cyber risk is a growing form of operational risk, necessitating advanced security measures and ongoing education.
Operational risk is harder to quantify and manage compared to credit or market risk.
Unlike insurance companies that can raise premiums to cover large losses, banks do not have this option as they operate in a competitive environment.


10.1.1 Defining Operational Risk

Basel Committee’s 2001 definition: “The risk of loss resulting from inadequate or failed internal processes, people, and systems or from external events.”
Legal risk is included, but reputation and strategic decision risks are excluded.
Operational risks affect bank costs and revenue, and can interact with credit and market risks.
E.g., natural disasters, political and regulatory risk, security breaches, and so on.



10.1.2 Types of Operational Risk

Internal Fraud: Intentional acts to defraud or circumvent law by internal parties. Examples include insider trading and rogue trading.
External Fraud: Fraudulent acts by third parties such as robbery and computer hacking.
Employment Practices and Workplace Safety: Violations related to employment laws, health and safety, including discrimination claims.
Clients, Products, and Business Practices: Failures to meet professional obligations or misuse of customer information. Examples include fiduciary breaches and money laundering.
Damage to Physical Assets: Losses from natural disasters or vandalism. Examples include fires and earthquakes.
Business Disruption and System Failures: Issues like hardware failures and utility outages disrupting business.
Execution, Delivery, and Process Management: Transaction failures, documentation issues, and vendor disputes.



10.1.3 Loss Severity and Loss Frequency

Loss frequency and loss severity are two fundamental distributions to estimate potential size of operational risks.
Loss Frequency: Distribution of the number of losses observed in one year. Commonly modeled using the Poisson distribution. \[
P(n) = e^{-\lambda T} \frac{(\lambda T)^n}{n!}
\]
Where \(\lambda\) is the average number of losses per year.
Loss Severity: Distribution of the size of a loss, given that a loss occurs. Often modeled using the lognormal distribution.
Monte Carlo Simulation: Used to combine loss frequency and severity to determine a total loss distribution.
Banks often have limited internal data but may utilize data from other banks or vendors.
Losses from other banks must be adjusted to reflect size of the institution.
Publicly reported data, like that from vendors, is often skewed towards larger losses.
Scenario analysis is used to assess operational risks that have not yet been observed.
Committee estimates for loss frequency and severity are subjective.
Scenarios can be developed to think actively about potential adverse events and formulate responses.



10.1.4 The Standardized Measurement Approach

Defined by regulators in December 2017, see www.bis.org/bcbs/publ/d424.pdf.
Implementation date: January 2023
Its purpose was to standardize the calculation of capital for operational risk in banks.
Business Indicator (BI) to measures the bank’s size and therefore its potential operational risk exposure:

Interest Leases and Dividend Component (ILDC)
Services Component (SC)
Financial Component (FC)
Formula: \(\text{BI} = \text{ILDC} + \text{SC} + \text{FC}\)

Business Indicator Component (BIC):

Small Banks: \(BIC = 0.12 \times BI\)
BI between 1 and 30 billion euros: \(BIC = 0.12 + 0.15 \times (BI-1)\)
BI above 30 billion euros**: \(BIC = 4.47 + 0.18 \times (BI-30)\)

Internal Loss Multiplier (ILM):

Loss Component (LC) is equal to 15 times average annual operational risk losses over previous 10 years. \[
ILM = \ln \left[ e - 1 + \left( \frac{LC}{BIC} \right)^{0.8} \right]
\]

Operational Risk Capital:
For Small Banks (BI < 1 billion euros): Equal to BIC.
For Other Banks: Equal to \(BIC \times ILM\).
National Supervisors’ Discretion: May set capital equal to BIC for all banks or use loss data for small banks.
If \(LC = BIC\), then \(ILM = 1\) and required capital is BIC.
Banks with operational risk losses higher than average will have \(ILM > 1\).
Banks with losses lower than average will have \(ILM < 1\).



10.1.5 Preventing Operational Risk Losses

Operational risk managers aim to prevent losses by studying failures at other banks.
Managers explore causal relationships between decisions and operational risk to identify preventive measures, such as modifying employee qualifications or upgrading systems.
Business unit managers identify operational risks, often using questionnaires and scorecards (risk control and self-assesment).
Key risk indicators act as early warning systems, tracking metrics like:

Staff turnover
Number of failed transactions
Number of positions filled by temps
Ratio of supervisors to staff
Number of open positions
Percentage of staff who did not take 10 days consecutive leave in the last 12 months




10.1.6 Allocation of Operational Risk Capital

Capital should be allocated in a way that motivates business units to manage operational risks effectively.
Reduction in operational risk should lead to less allocated capital, thereby increasing return on capital for the unit.
However, costs of reducing certain risks may outweigh the benefits.
Support from senior management and board oversight is critical.
Basel Committee on Banking Supervision recommends regular board reviews of risk management programs.



10.1.7 Insurance

Moral Hazard: Insuring against operational risks may encourage lax behavior in risk management.
Adverse Selection: Insurance companies may attract higher-risk clients, requiring rigorous assessment of bank controls.
Deductibles, coinsurance, and policy limits are used to manage these insurance risks.
crumbs: 10  Operational Risk, Liquidity Risk, and Model Risk

title: 10  Operational Risk, Liquidity Risk, and Model Risk
text:
10.2 Liquidity Risk

Elevated by the Global Financial Crisis of 2007.
Solvency refers to a company having more assets than liabilities, so that the value of its equity is positive.
Liquidity refers to the ability of a company to make cash payments as they become due.
Financial institutions that are solvent can fail because of liquidity problems.
Liquidity Funding Risk: Risk associated with inability to meet short-term obligations.
Liquidity Trading Risk: Concerns bid-ask spreads and asset marketability.
Regulatory liquidity requirements aim to ensure worst-case scenario survival.
“Liquidity Black Holes”: Extreme situations where market liquidity evaporates suddenly.


10.2.1 Liquidity Trading Risk

Factors Affecting Liquidity:

Asset type (e.g., IBM shares vs. non-investment-grade bonds)
Trade size
Speed of liquidation
Economic environment

Market makers may widen bid-ask spreads for large trades.
Without market makers, implicit bid-ask spreads exist, varying from 0.01% to 10% of mid-market price.
Market participants may anticipate large position unwinds, affecting liquidity.
Importance of maintaining confidentiality to avoid predatory trading.
Lack of transparency can lead to illiquid markets.
During the Global Financial Crisis, complex derivatives became illiquid due to lack of understanding.
“Liquidity Black Hole”: liquidity evaporates when multiple institutions try to unwind similar positions simultaneously.



10.2.2 Measuring Market Liquidity

Bid-Ask Spread: Measures liquidity either as a cash amount or as a proportion of the asset price.
Cash Bid-Ask Spread: \(p = \text{Ask price} - \text{Bid price}\)
Proportional Bid-Ask Spread: \(s = \frac{\text{Ask price} - \text{Bid
price}}{\text{Mid-market price}}\)
One measure of the liquidity of a book is how much it would cost to liquidate the book in normal market conditions within a certain time.
Cost of Liquidation: Estimates the cost to liquidate an asset or book of assets.
Cost of Liquidation (Normal Market):
\[
\text{Cost} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{s_i \alpha_i}{2}
\]
where \(s_i\) is the proportional bid-ask spread and \(\alpha_i\) is the value of the position.
Cost of Liquidation (Stressed Market):
\[
\text{Cost} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{(\mu_i + \lambda \sigma_i) \alpha_i}{2}
\]
\(\mu_i\) is the mean of the proportional bid-offer spread for the \(i\)th financial instrument.
\(\sigma_i\) is standard deviation of the proportional bid-offer spread for the \(i\)th financial instrument.
\(\lambda\) gives the required confidence level for the spread.
Diversification doesn’t necessarily reduce liquidity risk.
Monitoring liquidity regularly is crucial.
The measure is sensitive to the time period assumed for liquidation.
Holding many small positions rather than a few large positions therefore tends to entail less liquidity risk.
Setting limits to the size of any one position can therefore be one way of reducing liquidity trading risk.


10.2.2.1 Additional Notes

Liquidity-Adjusted VaR:

It incorporates both market risk and liquidity risk into a single metric (VaR plus Cost of liquidation).
It can be calculated using either normal market conditions or stressed market conditions.

Unwinding a Position Optimally:

A dilemma exists between liquidating quickly (higher bid-ask spread, less market risk) and liquidating slowly (lower bid-ask spread, higher market risk).
Almgren and Chriss (2001) provide a mathematical approach to optimize this process.

Other Measures of Market Liquidity:

Besides bid-ask spread, trading volume and price impact are also considered.
A metric proposed by Amihud relates daily return to daily dollar volume, suggesting that higher expected returns compensate for lower liquidity.
Amihud’s Measure: Average of \[ \frac{\text{Absolute value of daily return}}{\text{Daily dollar volume}} \]
This is a simpler but similar measure to price impact.
Amihud (2002) demonstrates that as liquidity decreases, an asset’s expected return increases, implying that investors are compensated for taking on liquidity risk.





10.2.3 Liquidity Funding Risk

Focuses on a financial institution’s capacity to meet cash needs.
Institutions can be solvent yet fail due to liquidity issues.
Causes of Liquidity Funding Problems:

Economic Liquidity Stresses: Reluctance from investors to fund any credit-risk bearing activities.
Aggressive Funding Decisions: Tendency to fund long-term needs with short-term instruments, causing liquidity mismatch.
Poor Financial Performance: Leads to loss of confidence, deposit withdrawal, and funding difficulties.

Often, all three causes may contribute to severe liquidity problems.
Managing Risk:

Predicting Cash Needs: Some are predictable (e.g., bond coupons), others less so (e.g., deposit withdrawals).
Complex Financial Instruments: Increase unpredictability of cash needs due to factors like downgrade triggers and counterparty defaults.

The key is to ensure cash needs can be met even in adverse scenarios.


10.2.3.1 Sources of Liquidity

Cash and Treasury Securities: Highly liquid but expensive, limiting the amount held.
Liquidating Trading Book: Requires monitoring liquidity under stressed market conditions.
Ability to Borrow: Easy during unstressed periods but difficult during market stress.
Retail and Wholesale Deposits: Volatile and dependent on market-wide conditions.
Securitization: Used to be reliable but became risky after 2007.
Central Bank Borrowing: Lender of last resort, subject to collateral and haircut.


Additional Considerations:

Stressed vs Normal Conditions: Different liquidity sources function differently under stress.
Collateral and Haircut: Important when borrowing from central banks.
Hedging Issues: May result in liquidity problems due to margin requirements.





10.2.4 Reserve Requirements and Regulation

A set percentage of a bank’s deposits must be kept in reserve.
Affects the money supply; varies by country.
Basel III Liquidity Risk Requirements:

LCR (Liquidity Coverage Ratio): High-quality liquid assets to net cash outflows in a 30-day stress period should be \(\geq\) 100%.
NSFR (Net Stable Funding Ratio): Amount of stable funding to required stable funding should be \(\geq\) 100%.




10.2.5 Liquidity Black Holes

A situation where market liquidity dries up due to everyone wanting to sell and no one wanting to buy, or vice versa.
It is still possible to buy/sell assets but the bid-ask spread is huge.
Negative Feedback Traders: Buy when prices fall, sell when prices rise.
Positive Feedback Traders: Do the opposite, exacerbating price movements.
Dominance of positive feedback traders leads to unstable, illiquid markets.
Reasons for positive feedback trading:

Trend Trading: Buy or sell based on asset price trends.
Stop-Loss Rules: Automatic selling below certain price levels.
Dynamic Hedging: Can reduce market liquidity when large short option positions are hedged.
Synthetic Options: Commercial programs for synthetic put options can cause liquidity black holes.
Margins: Big market moves lead to margin calls, further destabilizing prices.
Predatory Trading: Knowing an investor must sell large quantities can drive prices further down.
LTCM Failure: Example of how leveraging and margin calls can exacerbate liquidity issues.

Leveraging and Deleveraging:

Banks awash with liquidity make credit easily available, leading to increased asset prices (“leveraging”).
Deleveraging is the opposite: reduced liquidity in banks results in tightened credit and decreased asset prices.
Hedge funds are particularly affected by leveraging-deleveraging cycles.

Irrational Exuberance:

Term coined by Alan Greenspan in 1996 to describe overvaluation of asset prices.
Often self-reinforcing due to high short-term profits.
Leads to liquidity black holes and market crashes.

The Impact of Regulation:

Uniform regulatory environment can lead to synchronous behavior among banks, exacerbating market issues.
Different financial cycles necessitate different capital requirements; uniformity can be problematic.

The Importance of Diversity:

Market participants often do not act independently, causing liquidity black holes.
Different types of financial institutions should be regulated differently to introduce market diversity.
Contrarian investment strategies may offer benefits but are hard to implement under short-term VaR-based risk management.

title: 10  Operational Risk, Liquidity Risk, and Model Risk
text:
10.3 Model Risk Management

Models increasingly used for various applications like credit decisions, liquidity management, and risk assessment.
Advanced analytic techniques, including machine learning, automate tasks previously done by humans.
Large institutions require a model risk management function for appropriate use and validation.
Models are approximations, dependent on assumptions about the phenomena they represent.
Importance of understanding these assumptions and their limitations.
Model risk is considered a component of operational risk.
Banks must develop, validate, and use models in accordance with regulatory systems.


10.3.1 Regulatory Guidance

SR 11-7 by the U.S. Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System outlines principles for effective model risk management.
Model risk defined as adverse consequences from incorrect or misused models.
Model risk occurs primarily for two reasons:

The model may have fundamental errors (data, calculation)
The model may be used incorrectly or inappropriately (limitations and assumptions of the model)

Model Development:

Importance of thorough documentation for model continuity, regulatory scrutiny, and validation.
Testing should be rigorous, including out-of-sample tests and performance under extreme conditions.

Model Validation:

Model validation groups provide an independent check.
Three core elements: conceptual soundness, ongoing monitoring, and outcomes analysis.
Importance of independence and corporate culture.

Vendor Models:

Vendor models should undergo similar validation processes.
Require documentation and ongoing monitoring from vendors.
Contingency plans for vendor unavailability.




10.3.2 Models in Physics and Finance

Physical models are precise because they describe fundamental laws of nature.
Financial models are approximate, influenced by human behavior.
Parameters like gravitational pull in physics are constant.
Financial parameters like volatility change frequently.
Financial models can result in significant errors and losses.
Incorrect assumptions and lack of validation can be costly.
To manage model risk, monitoring trading patterns and competitiveness in various products is crucial.



10.3.3 Models for Pricing Actively Traded Products

A situation that is common in the over-the-counter market is one where a financial instrument that has to be valued is a standard product such as an option, but not exactly the same as one that trades in the market.
Actively traded financial instruments have market-determined prices.
Models like Black–Scholes–Merton are used for similar but non-standard products to ensure pricing consistency.
Models are primarily interpolation tools; changes in model usually don’t affect prices significantly.
Model risk exists in extreme market conditions and must be validated.
Hedging focuses on both within-model and outside-model variables.
Portfolio value changes can be attributed to unhedged risks, model imperfections, and new trades.
Risk is often diversified away in large financial institutions, minimizing the impact of inaccurate hedging.



10.3.4 Models for Less Actively Traded Products

For actively traded products, calibrating models to market prices minimizes model risk.
For less-traded products, model choice significantly affects valuation and hedging.
Institutions often aim for relative, not absolute, valuation based on similar market-traded instruments.
Multiple models should be used for non-standard products to understand model risk.
Using multiple models can provide a range of possible prices.
Institutions should charge at the higher end of this range and be conservative about recognizing profits.
Accounting standards categorize valuations as Level 1, 2, or 3 based on reliability and need for modeling assumptions.



10.3.5 Successful Pricing Models and Common Mistakes

The goal is to create models that aid in market understanding and decision-making without unnecessary complexity.
Successful Pricing Models:

Consistency: Aligns with observed market prices, useful for interpolation.
Communication: Facilitates stable, easier-to-understand metrics like implied volatilities.
Simplicity: Intuitive enough for traders to develop a nuanced understanding.

Model-Building Considerations:

Avoid Over-fitting: Complex models may capture data well but lack utility and might produce unrealistic outcomes.
Caution on Over-parameterization: Adding too many parameters complicates the model and could fail during market regime changes.
Preference for Simplicity: Simple models are often preferred as they are easier to understand and less of a ‘black box.’

title: 10  Operational Risk, Liquidity Risk, and Model Risk
text:
10.4 Practice Questions and Problems

10.4.1 Operational Risk

What risks are included by regulators in their definition of operational risks? What risks are not included?
Discuss how moral hazard and adverse selection are handled in car insurance.
When is a trading loss classified as a market risk and when is it classified as an operational risk?
Discuss whether there is (a) moral hazard and (b) adverse selection in life insurance contracts.
Give two examples of key risk indicators that might be monitored by a central operational risk management group within a bank.
Consider two banks, each with a BIC of 200 million euros. Bank A has suffered 10 operational risk losses of 20 million euros in the past 10 years. Bank B has suffered one 200 million euro loss in the past 10 years. What is the operational risk regulatory capital for each bank under SMA?



10.4.2 Liquidity Risk

An asset is quoted bid 50, offer 55. What does this mean? What is the proportional bid–offer spread?
Suppose that an investor has shorted shares worth $5,000 of Company A and bought shares worth $3,000 of Company B. The proportional bid–offer spread for Company A is 0.01 and the proportional bid–offer spread for Company B is 0.02. What does it cost the investor to unwind the portfolio?
Suppose that in Problem 2. the bid–offer spreads for the two companies are normally distributed. For Company A the bid–offer spread has a mean of 0.01 and a standard deviation of 0.01. For Company B the bid–offer spread has a mean of 0.02 and a standard deviation of 0.03. What is the cost of unwinding that the investor is 95% confident will not be exceeded?
Explain the difference between the liquidity coverage ratio (LCR) and the net stable funding ratio (NSFR).
Why is it risky to rely on wholesale deposits for funding?
What is meant by (a) positive feedback trading and (b) negative feedback trading? Which is liable to lead to liquidity problems?
What is meant by liquidity-adjusted VaR?
Explain how liquidity black holes occur. How can regulation lead to liquidity black holes?
Why is it beneficial to the liquidity of markets for traders to follow diverse trading strategies?



10.4.3 Model Risk Management

How is a financial institution likely to find that it is using a model different from its competitors for valuing a particular type of derivatives product?
Distinguish between within-model and outside-model hedging.
What is meant by P&L decomposition?
What is meant by over-fitting and over-parameterization in model building?
“For structured products, traders mark to model. They do not mark to market.” Explain this remark.

title: 11  Climate Risk and Enterprise Risk Management
text: 11.1 Climate Risk, ESG, and Sustainability
crumbs: 11  Climate Risk and Enterprise Risk Management

title: 11  Climate Risk and Enterprise Risk Management
text:
Environmental risks pose a threat to Earth’s habitability for future generations.
Companies and financial institutions are under increasing societal pressure to address these risks.
The prominence of environmental risks is expected to rise in the coming years.
Climate risk is identified as the most significant environmental risk.


11.1.1 Climate Risk

Climate risk is one of the most pressing environmental concern.
Human activities emit tens of billions of tons of greenhouse gases annually.
Greenhouse gases, primarily CO2, persist for hundreds of years, exacerbating global warming.
Paris Agreement (2015) and Glasgow Climate Pact (2021) aim for net-zero emissions by





Uncertainties exist in achieving these goals due to varying global policy responses.
Difficulty in balancing current emissions with future environmental damage (trade-off between emissions today and damage in the future).
Major sectors contributing to greenhouse gas emissions in the US are transportation, electricity, and industry.
China, U.S., and India as major emitters, with per capita disparities. https://www.worldometers.info/co2-emissions/co2-emissions-by-country/
Climate change causes rising sea levels due to ice melt and water expansion.
Increase in extreme weather events like storms, heatwaves, and hurricanes.
Extreme events already cost billions; e.g., Hurricane Ida cost $75 billion in 2021.


11.1.1.1 Physical and Transition Risks

Analysts differentiate between physical and transition climate risks.
Physical risks involve costs from extreme climate events like floods and hurricanes.
Transition risks are linked to policies for lower emissions, such as carbon taxes.
Companies may need to shut down fossil fuel plants earlier due to transition risks.
Lower emissions also present new business opportunities in green technologies.
Estimated $100–150 trillion needed for economic transition over next 30 years.
Carbon tax and cap-and-trade systems as tools to regulate emissions, varying adoption globally.



11.1.1.2 Insurance Companies

Climate change significantly impacts the insurance industry by affecting risk correlations.
Traditional “law of large numbers” less applicable due to correlated climate-related disasters.
Short-term contracts allow pricing adjustments.
Life insurance less adaptable to climate risks.
Resource allocation needed for better climate risk understanding and regulatory compliance.
Companies increasingly reluctant to insure high-risk areas for natural disasters.



11.1.1.3 Banks

Banks actively quantify climate change effects through forums like PCAF (Partnership for Carbon Accounting Financials).
Regulatory bodies launch climate stress tests for banks.
Climate risks impact multiple traditional risk categories:

Credit Risk: Climate risk can impair debt service and collateral value.
Market Risk: Asset prices may decline due to climate effects.
Liquidity Risk: Market changes may limit access to stable funding.
Operational Risk: Legal and compliance risks may rise.
Reputational Risk: Public sentiment can heighten reputational risks.

Mortgages vulnerable to long-term climate change impacts on real estate values.
Severe climate events can reduce corporate profitability, raising credit risk.
Mass migrations and agricultural changes expected, affecting economic stability.
Sovereign debt increasingly risky, particularly in less-developed countries.
Transition to low-carbon economy creates winners and losers, affecting lending.
Reputational risks make banks cautious in lending to carbon emitters.



11.1.1.4 Institutional Investors

Increasing focus on incorporating climate risk in portfolios.
Companies incentivized to align with emission targets to attract institutional investments.
Green Bonds: Innovative financial instruments designated for climate-friendly investments, underwritten by investment banks.
Multiple organizations aim to standardize climate-related financial reporting, but efforts are still in progress.
Asset managers like BlackRock are proactive, while others like Berkshire Hathaway are resistant.
Political Counterpressures: Legislation proposals and requests to ignore environmental factors in state pension investments.
Despite political setbacks, focus on emissions and climate risk disclosure is growing.




11.1.2 ESG

Shift from profit-centric approach to incorporating environmental, social, and governance (ESG) factors.
Social Aspect: Addresses diversity, equity, inclusion, and worker well-being. Metrics still under development.
Governance Aspect: Focuses on transparent accounting and accountability to shareholders. Avoids conflicts of interest and unethical behavior.
Multiple ESG data providers exist, often yielding conflicting reports.
Absence of regulated ESG reporting standards allows companies to shop for favorable scores, creating risk for investors.



11.1.3 Sustainability

Sustainability Defined: Meets current needs without jeopardizing future generations.
Examples of Nonsustainability: Greenhouse gas emissions, ocean pollution, soil and water contamination.
Sustainability Accounting Standards Board (SASB) and Global Reporting Initiative provide guidelines.
Three Pillars:

Environmental Sustainability: Resource conservation.
Economic Sustainability: Long-term stability with social and environmental care.
Social Sustainability: Supports healthy, livable communities for current and future generations.

Sustainable investment funds aim to invest in companies with good ESG or sustainability practices.
No significant outperformance compared to traditional funds, despite increased demand. https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/abs/10.1111/jofi.12841
Some reports overstate benefits, suggest high returns with low risks contrary to finance theory.



11.1.4 Greenwashing

Greenwashing Concept: Companies misleadingly claim ecological responsibility to attract consumers.
Examples:

Innocent drinks: False claims about recyclable plastics.
Keurig: Misleading recycling claims led to a $3 million fine.
IKEA: Used illegally sourced wood despite good sustainability credentials.
Ryanair: Unsubstantiated claim of being Europe’s “lowest emissions airline.”

Financial Institutions: Deutsche Bank’s DWS exaggerated green credentials, leading to CEO’s resignation.
Implications: Considered a serious offense, impacting consumer trust and corporate credibility.
crumbs: 11  Climate Risk and Enterprise Risk Management

title: 11  Climate Risk and Enterprise Risk Management
text:
11.2 Enterprise Risk Management

Risk managers must evaluate risks and adopt a holistic view through Enterprise Risk Management (ERM).
Top-down and bottom-up approaches essential for effective risk management.

Top-down sets overall risk appetite.
Bottom-up assesses risks at the business unit level.

Shift from compliance orientation to strategic orientation in risk management is noted.


11.2.1 Risk Appetite

How much risk is the organization prepared to take in order to achieve its strategic objectives and business plans?
Risk appetite is an essential part of Enterprise Risk Management (ERM).
For fund managers, risk appetite is more straightforward to quantify using beta, expected return, and risk.
For fund managers it is possible to use \(\beta\) that is consistent with a particular VaR objective.

\[
\beta = \frac{R - R_F}{R_M - R_F + N^{-1} (1-p) \sigma_M}
\]

\(R\) is the return that has a probability \(p\) of being exceeded in one year.
\(R_M\) is the expected market return and \(R_F\) is the risk-free rate.
Unfortunately, the available risk-return alternatives for a financial institution such as a bank or an insurance company cannot usually be quantified as easily as for a fund manager.
VaR or expected shortfall often specify worst-case loss aspect of risk appetite.
Other elements like reputational risk, credit rating risk, legal risks, etc., may be qualitative.
Risk quantification can be:

Bottom-Up: Calculated by business units, then aggregated.
Top-Down: Set by the board, guiding risk limits for individual business lines.

Risk appetite should address risk Concentration: Limit exposure to sectors, market variables, companies.
Monitoring and adherence to set limits crucial for effectiveness.



11.2.2 Risk Culture

It is all about how decisions are made.
If the risk culture is good, all decisions are made in a disciplined way with a careful consideration of possible outcomes, weighing risks and rewards.
A strong risk culture should align with an institution’s risk appetite and long-term objectives.
Short-term profits should not outweigh long-term risks.
The role of compensation in influencing decisions is crucial and is being revised to mitigate short-term focus.
E.g., spreading bonuses over years, clawback provisions for adverse outcomes.
Regulatory guidelines (Financial Stability Board) can assist in establishing a robust risk culture.



11.2.3 Identifying Major Risks

Core to ERM, similar to stress testing; consider historical and emerging trends.
Regulatory Changes: Banks face tighter regulations; shift of activities to less-regulated shadow banks.
Cognitive Biases:

Wishful Thinking: Confusing desires with likely outcomes.
Anchoring: Fixation on initial estimates, neglecting full range of outcomes.
Availability: Overemphasis on recent information.
Representativeness: Stereotyping situations based on past experiences.
Sunk Costs Bias: Ignoring sunk costs critical for rational decision-making.

Underestimating tail risks due to cognitive biases; requires methods to improve estimations.
Inclusion of diverse views mitigates bias, improves risk identification.



11.2.4 Strategic Risk Management

Focuses on assessing assumptions and potential failures in a company’s strategy.

Key Questions: Impact of competitors, customer reactions, and external events.
Risk is tied to the poor performance under ‘wrong’ scenarios.

Courses of Action Once Risk Identified:

Avoid: Terminate the business or investment.
Reduce: Mitigate likelihood or impact of adverse events.
Adjust: Modify plans to lower risks.
Share/Insure: Transfer or share some risk.
Accept: No action taken.

Choice of action varies by context but requires thorough consideration.

title: 11  Climate Risk and Enterprise Risk Management
text:
11.3 Practice Questions and Problems

11.3.1 Climate Risk, ESG, and Sustainability

Why does the burning of fossil fuels lead to temperature rises?
What is the goal that was agreed to in Paris in 2015?
What is the difference between a carbon tax and a cap and trade system?
What is the difference between physical risks and transition risks?
What are the risks of climate change to insurance companies?
What does ESG stand for?
What is greenwashing?
How can pension plans and mutual funds help solve the global warming problem?
Give examples of activities covered by ESG and sustainability that are not climate risk issues.



11.3.2 Enterprise Risk Management

Explain the difference between top-down and bottom-up approaches to risk management. Why are both necessary in ERM?
“The risk culture at a bank should lead it to consider the longer-term implications of decisions as well as short-term profits.” Discuss.
What steps can a bank take to encourage employees to consider more than short-term profits?
What distinguishes enterprise risk management from more traditional approaches to risk management?
A fund’s risk appetite is such that it wants to be 90% certain that it will not lose more than 20% in any one year. Using the performance of the S&P 500 between 1970 and 2016 (mean return 11.74%, standard deviation 18.86%), determine the beta the fund should have. Assume a risk-free rate of 3% per annum.
Give three examples of cognitive biases in finance.

title: 12  Regulation, Economic Capital, and RAROC
text: 12.1 Basel I, Basel II, and Solvency II
crumbs: 12  Regulation, Economic Capital, and RAROC

title: 12  Regulation, Economic Capital, and RAROC
text:
The Basel Accord of 1988 initiated international standards for bank regulation.
Regulatory evolution has seen new rules modify old ones while retaining certain approaches.
Understanding history is crucial for grasping the current regulatory landscape.
The chapter outlines Basel I, netting provisions, and the 1996 Amendment.
Basel II, introduced around 2007, significantly reformed the regulatory framework.
Solvency II, akin to Basel II, was adopted by the European Union in 2016 for insurance companies.


12.1.1 The Reasons for Regulating Banks

The primary aim of bank regulation is to ensure sufficient capital against risks.
Governments aim to minimize the probability of bank defaults for economic stability.
Self-regulation by banks is not historically supported; formal regulation enhances capital and risk awareness.
Deposit insurance programs, while protecting depositors, can inadvertently encourage risk-taking (“moral hazard”).
Systemic risk is a major concern, as a default by one large bank can trigger a financial system collapse.
Governments face a dilemma between bailing out struggling institutions and risking systemic collapse.
The 2008 market turmoil involved both bailouts and allowed failures, exemplified by Lehman Brothers, to send market signals.



12.1.2 Bank Regulation Pre-1988

Pre-1988 bank regulation focused on setting minimum capital-to-asset ratios within individual countries.
Regulatory stringency and definitions varied globally, leading to competitive imbalances.
Major international loans and complex transactions, like OTC derivatives, exposed the inadequacy of existing capital requirements.
Basel Committee formed in 1974 to standardize regulations.
1988 BIS Accord, later Basel I, was the Committee’s first major output.



12.1.3 The 1988 BIS Accord

First international risk-based standards for capital adequacy.
Signed by all 12 Basel Committee members.
Increased resources for risk measurement and management in banks.
The Accord set the groundwork for modern risk management but faced criticisms for being too simplistic.
Cooke Ratio: Focused on total risk-weighted assets (credit risk exposures).
Categories of Credit Risk Exposures:

On-Balance Sheet Assets: Excludes derivatives.
Off-Balance Sheet Items: Excludes derivatives.
Over-the-Counter Derivatives: Includes swaps, forwards, etc.



12.1.3.1 Key Equations:

Risk-Weighted Assets (On-Balance): \[
\sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i L_i
\]
where \(L_i\) is the principal amount and \(w_i\) is its risk weight.
Credit Equivalent Amount (Off-Balance):
Conversion factors applied to principal amounts of instruments like bankers’ acceptances, guarantees, and loan commitments.
Credit Equivalent Amount (Derivatives): \[
\text{max}(V, 0) + aL
\]
where \(V\) is current value, \(a\) is an add-on factor, and \(L\) is the principal amount.
Total Risk-Weighted Assets: \[
\sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i L_i + \sum_{j=1}^{M} w^*_j C_j
\]
where \(C_j\) is the credit equivalent amount for off-balance-sheet items, and \(w^*_j\) is the risk weight of the counterparty.



12.1.3.2 Capital Requirements

Required banks to maintain capital equal to at least 8% of total risk-weighted assets.
Tier 1 Capital:

Includes equity and noncumulative perpetual preferred stock.
Goodwill is subtracted from equity.
Must constitute at least 50% of required capital (i.e., 4% of risk-weighted assets).
Must include 2% of risk-weighted assets as common equity.

Tier 2 Capital:

Also known as Supplementary Capital.
Includes cumulative perpetual preferred stock, certain 99-year debentures, and subordinated debt with original life over five years.
Provides cushion for depositors in case of insolvency.

Some countries and banks maintain capital ratios above the Basel Committee’s minimum requirements.




12.1.4 Netting

Standard in over-the-counter (OTC) derivatives markets.
Netting clause stating that all transactions are a single transaction in case of default.
Netting effectively reduces credit risk and modifies regulatory capital requirements.
Basel Accords: Modified in 1995 to include netting.
Without Netting: Loss to the bank = Sum of positive value transactions.

Example: Bank’s transactions worth +$24M, -$17M, +$8M. Loss = $24M + $8M = $32M.

With Netting: Loss to the bank = Sum of all transactions.

Example: Loss = $24M - $17M + $8M = $15M.

General Formula:

Without Netting: \(\sum_{i=1}^{N} \max(V_i, 0)\)
With Netting: \(\max(\sum_{i=1}^{N} V_i, 0)\)




12.1.5 The 1996 Amendment

The 1996 Amendment to the 1988 Basel Accord added capital charges for market risks in trading activities.
It mandated fair value accounting, also known as marking to market, for a bank’s trading book.
Two approaches for calculating market risk: standardized and internal model-based.
Value-at-risk (VaR) metrics and Specific Risk Charge (SRC) introduced.
Total capital combines credit and market risk, with Tier options.
Back-testing required; multiplier adjusted based on model performance.



12.1.6 Basel II

Follow-up to the 1988 Basel Accord to address its weaknesses.
Overall Goal: More sophisticated and comprehensive regulatory framework to manage risks in banking.
Initial proposal in June 1999, revised in January 2001 and April 2003.
Final rules published in June 2004, updated in November 2005.
Implementation started in 2007.
U.S.: Applies to internationally active banks; small banks follow Basel IA.
EU: Applies to all banks and securities companies.


12.1.6.1 Three Pillars of Basel II

Minimum Capital Requirements:


More risk-sensitive approach to credit risk.
Operational risk included.
Market risk remains unchanged.
8% of risk-weighted assets (RWA) still the base requirement.


Supervisory Review:


Emphasizes qualitative and quantitative risk management.
Regulatory flexibility but seeks global consistency.
Focuses on early intervention.


Market Discipline:


Greater disclosure requirements.
Aims to improve risk management through transparency.

\[
\text{Total Capital} = 0.08 \times (\text{credit risk RWA} + \text{market risk RWA} +
\text{operational risk RWA})
\]



12.1.7 Solvency II

Solvency II is the regulatory framework for insurance companies in the European Union, replacing Solvency I in 2016.
Unlike Solvency I, which focused solely on underwriting risks, Solvency II also considers investment and operational risks.
Structure resembles Basel II with three pillars:

Pillar 1: Minimum Capital Requirements (MCR) and Solvency Capital Requirement (SCR) calculated. Breach triggers supervisory intervention.
Pillar 2: Supervisory review process for internal capital adequacy.
Pillar 3: Disclosure of risk management information to the market.

SCR can be calculated using the standardized approach or internal models involving Value-at-Risk (VaR) with a one-year time horizon and 99.5% confidence limit.
SCR includes charges for investment risk (market and credit), underwriting risk (life, non-life, health), and operational risk.
Capital adequacy tests include various adverse event scenarios (e.g., 32% stock market decrease, 20% real estate price decrease).
Internal models must pass three tests: statistical quality, calibration, and use.
Types of capital:

Tier 1: Equity capital, retained earnings.
Tier 2: Liabilities subordinated to policyholders, available in wind-down scenarios.
Tier 3: Liabilities subordinated to policyholders, not meeting Tier 2 criteria.

Capital composition rules are specified for Tier 1, Tier 1 + Tier 2, and Tier 1 + Tier 2 + Tier 3.
crumbs: 12  Regulation, Economic Capital, and RAROC

title: 12  Regulation, Economic Capital, and RAROC
section: 12.2 Basel II.5, Basel III, and Other Post-Crisis Changes
text:
12.2 Basel II.5, Basel III, and Other Post-Crisis Changes

Basel II’s implementation roughly coincided with the onset of the Global Financial Crisis.
Critics argue Basel II encouraged self-regulation, allowing banks to use their own model inputs for regulatory capital.
Basel II may not be solely to blame:

The origins of the crisis predate Basel II.
U.S. was late in implementing Basel II, affecting capital levels.

Basel II.5 was introduced post-crisis to amend market risk capital calculations.

Implementation date: December 31, 2011.

Basel III was a more comprehensive post-crisis regulatory overhaul.

First set of regulations: December 2010.
Finalized: December 2017.
Implementation phased over several years.

Other post-2008 regulations:

Dodd–Frank Act in the U.S., signed July 21, 2010.
New rules in the European Union and the United Kingdom.



12.2.1 Basel II.5

Implemented on December 31, 2011, it introduced changes to the calculation of market risk capital.
Three Main Changes:

Stressed Value-at-Risk (VaR)
Incremental Risk Charge (IRC)
Comprehensive Risk Measure (CRM)

Impact: Significantly increased the capital large banks need to hold for market risk.


12.2.1.1 Details:

Stressed VaR:

Originally based on 10-day, 99% VaR.
Introduces a stressed VaR, based on 250-day stressed market conditions. \[
\text{Total Capital Charge} = \max(VaR_{t-1}, m_c \times VaR_{\text{avg}}) +
\max(sVaR_{t-1}, m_s \times sVaR_{\text{avg}})
\]
\(VaR_{t-1}\) and \(sVaR_{t-1}\) are the Value-at-Risk and stressed Value-at-Risk calculated on the previous day with a 10-day time horizon and a 99% confidence level.
\(VaR_{\text{avg}}\) and \(sVaR_{\text{avg}}\) are the averages of Value-at-Risk and stressed Value-at-Risk calculated over the previous 60 days with the same confidence level and time horizon.
\(m_c\) and \(m_s\) are multiplicative factors determined by bank supervisors, with a minimum value of three.
Effect: At least doubles the previous capital requirement.

Incremental Risk Charge (IRC):

Addresses discrepancy between trading and banking book capital requirements.
Calculates a one-year 99.9% VaR, considering both credit rating changes and defaults.
Includes a “liquidity horizon” for each instrument.
Employs the “constant level of risk assumption.”

Comprehensive Risk Measure (CRM):

Focuses on correlation-dependent instruments like ABSs and CDOs.
Replaces the Incremental Risk Charge and specific risk charge for these instruments.
Includes both standardized and internal model-based approaches.





12.2.2 Basel III

Introduced post-2007–2009 Global Financial Crisis to overhaul Basel II.
First set of regulations published in December 2010, finalized in 2017.
Implementation delayed to January 2023.
Six Key Components:

Capital Definition and Requirements
Capital Conservation Buffer
Countercyclical Buffer
Leverage Ratio
Liquidity Risk
Counterparty Credit Risk



12.2.2.1 Capital Definition and Requirements

Consists of Tier 1 equity capital, Additional Tier 1 capital, and Tier 2 capital. No Tier 3 capital.
Each tier has different characteristics and is intended to absorb losses to varying degrees, thereby contributing to a bank’s overall financial stability.


Tier 1 Equity Capital (Common Equity Tier 1 or CET1)

Includes share capital and retained earnings.
Excludes goodwill, deferred tax assets, and defined benefit pension plan deficits.

Additional Tier 1 (AT1) Capital

Items like non-cumulative preferred stock.
Previously considered Tier 1 but not common equity.

Tier 2 Capital

Includes subordinated debt with an original maturity of at least five years.



Capital Requirements:

Tier 1 equity capital: At least 4.5% of risk-weighted assets.
Total Tier 1 capital: At least 6% of risk-weighted assets.
Total capital (Tier 1 + Tier 2): At least 8% of risk-weighted assets.




12.2.2.2 Capital Conservation Buffer

Further CET1 capital equal to 2.5% of risk-weighted assets.
Dividend restrictions based on capital ratios.
To ensure banks accumulate additional common equity Tier 1 (CET1) capital during normal economic conditions.
This buffer serves as a reserve for absorbing losses during financial stress.
Banks must maintain a CET1 capital conservation buffer equal to 2.5% of risk-weighted assets.
Dividend Restrictions: If the buffer is partially or wholly used, dividend payouts are constrained until the capital is replenished.



12.2.2.3 Countercyclical Buffer

To counteract the cyclicality of bank earnings and protect the financial system during economic downturns.
The buffer can range from 0% to 2.5% of a bank’s total risk-weighted assets and must be met with Tier 1 equity capital (CET1).
The implementation level is determined by each country’s regulatory authorities, allowing for regional economic conditions.



12.2.2.4 Leverage Ratio

Purpose: To limit banks’ discretion in calculating risk-weighted assets and to provide additional information to regulators.
Potential Issue: Could encourage banks to hold riskier assets if this becomes the constraining ratio.
The leverage ratio is the ratio of Tier 1 capital to the exposure measure.
Basel III specifies a minimum leverage ratio of 3%.
Capital Measure: Total Tier 1 capital.
Exposure Measure: Sum of (a) on-balance-sheet exposures, (b) derivatives exposures, (c) securities financing transaction exposures, and (d) off-balance-sheet items.
No risk-weighting adjustments are made.
Some countries have proposed or implemented higher minimums.



12.2.2.5 Liquidity Risk

Basel III introduces the Liquidity Coverage Ratio (LCR) and the Net Stable Funding Ratio (NSFR).
LCR: Focuses on a bank’s ability to survive a 30-day liquidity disruption. \[
\text{LCR} = \frac{\text{High-Quality Liquid Assets}}{\text{Net Cash Outflows in a
30-Day Period}}
\]
NSFR: Focuses on liquidity management over one year. \[
\text{NSFR} = \frac{\text{Amount of Stable Funding}}{\text{Required Amount of Stable
Funding}}
\]
Liquidity Management: Designed to ensure that banks can survive liquidity pressures.



12.2.2.6 Counterparty Credit Risk

Banks calculate a Credit Value Adjustment (CVA) for each derivatives counterparty.
CVA measures expected loss from counterparty default.
Changes in CVA can be due to shifts in risk factors or credit spreads.
Basel III includes CVA risk in market risk capital requirements.



12.2.2.7 G-SIBs, SIFIs, and D-SIBs

Regulators aim to ensure that large, systemically important institutions hold sufficient capital.
G-SIBs: Global Systemically Important Banks
SIFIs: Systemically Important Financial Institutions (includes both banks and non-banks).
Importance is determined through scoring methodologies and other approaches like network theory.
G-SIBs required to hold extra Tier 1 equity capital, based on categorization.
Financial Stability Board publishes annual G-SIB list.
Total Loss-Absorbing Capacity (TLAC) rules have been set for G-SIBs.
Non-compliance with TLAC can limit a bank’s dividend issuance.
Some countries impose stricter capital requirements (e.g., Switzerland).
Domestic Systemically Important Banks (D-SIBs) have their own sets of rules and capital requirements.




12.2.3 Contingent Convertible Bonds (CoCos)

CoCos are bonds that automatically convert into equity under certain conditions, generally when a bank faces financial difficulties.
Offer higher interest rates than regular debt.
Benefits for banks: High return on equity in normal times, and an equity cushion during financial stress.
Benefits for regulators: Reduces the need for public bailouts.
Specifying the trigger for conversion, often based on Tier 1 equity capital ratios.



12.2.4 Dodd–Frank Act

The Dodd-Frank Wall Street Reform and Consumer Protection Act was signed into U.S. law in July 2010.
Aimed to prevent future bailouts and protect consumers.
Its primary objectives are to:

Enhance regulation and oversight of financial markets and institutions.
Reduce systemic risks in the financial sector.
Protect consumers through the creation of the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB).
Increase transparency in financial products and services.
crumbs: 12  Regulation, Economic Capital, and RAROC

title: 12  Regulation, Economic Capital, and RAROC
section: 12.3 Fundamental Review of the Trading Book
text:
12.3 Fundamental Review of the Trading Book

FRTB was introduced by the Basel Committee in May 2012, final rules published in January 2019, implementation slated for January 2023, but delays are expected.
Previous Approaches:

Basel I used Value at Risk (VaR) with a 99% confidence level.
Basel II.5 added “stressed VaR” based on historical periods of financial stress.

Key Features of FRTB:
Shifts from VaR to Expected Shortfall (ES) with a 97.5% confidence level (and stressed ES).
Liquidity horizons introduced, varying from 10 to 120 days, based on the risk factor considered.
Stricter definitions to counter regulatory arbitrage between the trading book and the banking book.
The Basel Committee outlines two approaches for banks: Standardized Approach and Internal Models Approach.
Banks approved for Internal Models Approach must also implement the Standardized Approach.
Standardized Approach calculates regulatory capital using delta, vega, and gamma exposures in the trading book.
Internal Models Approach uses stressed expected shortfall for regulatory capital calculation.
Calculations are performed separately for each trading desk.


12.3.1 Standardized Approach

Capital requirement is a sum of risk charge, default risk charge, and residual risk add-on.
Risk Charge:

Seven Risk Classes: General interest rate, foreign exchange, commodity, equity, and three credit spread risks.
Types of Risk Charges: Delta, Vega, and Curvature.

Default Risk Charge:

Calculated by Loss Given Default (LGD) and a default risk weight.

Residual Risk Add-On:

For risks not captured by delta/vega/curvature approach, e.g., exotic options.

Simplified Approach:

For smaller banks; excludes vega and gamma risks.




12.3.2 Trading Book vs. Banking Book

Trading Book: Contains financial instruments intended for trading. Subject to market risk capital.
Banking Book: Consists of instruments intended to be held to maturity. Subject to credit risk capital.
Regulatory Arbitrage: Past practices allowed banks to choose book placement based on advantageous capital requirements.
FRTB aims to clarify and standardize criteria for book classification.
‘Intent to trade’ insufficient; must be manageable on a trading desk and pose risks to solvency.
Instruments are initially assigned to a specific book with limited ability to transfer later.
Allow for book transfers, but any capital benefits from such moves are disallowed.
crumbs: 12  Regulation, Economic Capital, and RAROC

title: 12  Regulation, Economic Capital, and RAROC
text:
12.4 Economic Capital and RAROC

Economic Capital: Internal estimate of the capital a financial institution requires for its risk exposure.
It varies from regulatory capital, which follows standard rules.
Acts as a “currency” for risk-taking within an institution.


12.4.1 Definition of Economic Capital

Definition: Amount of capital needed to absorb losses over one year at a specific confidence level, tailored to maintain credit ratings like AA or BBB.
Economic capital fills the gap between expected loss and unexpected loss.
Case Example: An AA-rated bank with expected annual losses of 1% and a one-year 99.98% VaR of 5% would require $4 in economic capital per $100 of loans.
Approaches to Measure Economic Capital:

Top-Down: Uses asset volatility to estimate risk, often using Merton’s model.
Bottom-Up: Aggregates loss distributions by risk types and business units.

Risk-Adjusted Return on Capital (RAROC):

Measures profitability relative to allocated economic capital.
Used for assessing past and forecasting future performance, also aids in capital allocation decisions.

Types of Risks Considered:

Regulatory Capital covers Credit, Market, and Operational risks.
Some institutions also consider Business risk, which is not mandated for regulatory capital.




12.4.2 Components of Economic Capital

Market Risk Economic Capital:

Time horizon is usually one year; confidence level can go up to 99.98%.
Standard deviation for one-year loss or gain derived from annualized daily metrics.

Credit Risk Economic Capital:

Banks have freedom to choose their credit correlation models.
Popular approaches include CreditMetrics and Credit Risk Plus.
Can be conditional (cycle-specific) or unconditional (cycle-neutral) based on economic conditions.

Operational Risk Economic Capital:

Regulatory procedure often adopted; some banks use more advanced models.
Aims to create a complete distribution of losses.

Business Risk Economic Capital:

Involves strategic and reputational risks.
Difficult to quantify, largely subjective.
Quantification can inform the impact of new strategic initiatives on total risk.

Characteristics of Loss Distributions for Different Risk Types





Standard Deviation
Skewness
Kurtosis




Market risk
High
Zero
Low


Credit risk
Moderate
Moderate
Moderate


Operational risk
Low
High
High





12.4.3 Aggregating Economic Capital

To calculate the total economic capital for a financial institution by aggregating various types of risks such as market, credit, and operational risks.


Simple Summation (Basel Committee Method)

Formula: \(E_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} E_i\)
Assumes perfect correlation between risks.
Overestimates capital requirement by approx. 40%.

Normal Distributions

Formula: \(\sigma_{\text{total}} = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} \sigma_i
\sigma_j \rho_{ij}}\)
Underestimates capital due to disregard for skewness and kurtosis.

Using Copulas

Maps loss distributions to a standard distribution.
Gaussian copula as the simplest case.

Hybrid Approach

Formula: \(E_{\text{total}} = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} E_i E_j
\rho_{ij}}\)
Offers a balance between underestimation and overestimation of capital.




12.4.4 RAROC

Risk-adjusted performance measurement (RAPM) is crucial for assessing business units.
RAROC (Risk-Adjusted Return on Capital) is a widely-used approach.
Compares expected return with economic capital, adjusted for risk.

\[
\text{RAROC} = \frac{\text{Revenues} - \text{Costs} - \text{Expected
losses}}{\text{Economic capital}}
\]

Numerator can be pre-tax or post-tax.
An alternative includes adding a risk-free rate of return to the numerator.
Ex-ante calculations: For business expansion/contraction decisions.
Ex-post calculations: For performance evaluation and bonuses.
Decisions should not solely be based on ex-post results; consider long-term expectations.

title: 12  Regulation, Economic Capital, and RAROC
text:
12.5 Practice Questions and Problems

12.5.1 Basel I, Basel II, and Solvency II

“When a steel company goes bankrupt, other companies in the same industry benefit because they have one less competitor. But when a bank goes bankrupt other banks do not necessarily benefit.” Explain this statement.
“The existence of deposit insurance makes it particularly important for there to be regulations on the amount of capital banks hold.” Explain this statement.
An interest rate swap involves the exchange of a fixed rate of interest for a floating rate of interest with both being applied to the same principal. The principals are not exchanged. What is the nature of the credit risk for a bank when it enters into a five-year interest rate swap with a notional principal of $100 million? Assume the swap is worth zero initially.
In a currency swap, interest on a principal in one currency is exchanged for interest on a principal in another currency. The principals in the two currencies are exchanged at the end of the life of the swap. Why is the credit risk on a currency swap greater than that on an interest rate swap?
A four-year interest rate swap currently has a negative value to a financial institution. Is the financial institution exposed to credit risk on the transaction? Explain your answer.
All the derivatives transactions a bank has with a corporate client have a positive value to the bank. What is the value to the bank of netting provisions in its master agreement with the client?
What is the difference between the trading book and the banking book for a bank? A bank currently has a loan of $10 million to a corporate client. At the end of the life of the loan, the client would like to sell debt securities to the bank instead of borrowing. How does this potentially affect the nature of the bank’s regulatory capital calculations?
Under Basel I, banks do not like lending to highly creditworthy companies and prefer to help them issue debt securities. Why is this? Do you think this changed as a result of Basel II?
Banks sometimes use what is referred to as regulatory arbitrage to reduce their capital. What do you think this means?



12.5.2 Basel II.5, Basel III, and Oter Post-Crisis Changes

What are the three major components of Basel II.5?
What are the six major components of Basel III?
What is the difference between VaR as it has been traditionally measured and stressed VaR?
Explain how the incremental risk charge is calculated. Why was it introduced by the Basel Committee?
Explain how CoCo bonds work. Why are they attractive to (a) banks and (b) regulators?



12.5.3 Fundamental Review of the Trading Book

Outline the differences between the way market risk capital is calculated in (a) Basel I, (b) Basel II.5, and (c) the FRTB.
What are the advantages of expected shortfall over value at risk?
What is the difference between the trading book and the banking book? Why are regulators concerned about specifying whether an instrument should be one or the other in the FRTB?



12.5.4 Economic Capital and RAROC

What is the difference between economic capital and regulatory capital?
What determines the confidence level used by a AA-rated financial institution in its economic capital calculations?
What types of risk are included in business risk?
A bank is considering expanding its asset management operations. The main risk is operational risk. It estimates the expected operational risk loss from the new venture in one year to be $2 million and the 99.97% VaR (arising from a small chance of an large investor lawsuit) to be $40 million. The expected fees it will receive from investors for the funds under administration are $12 million per year, and administrative costs are expected to be $3 million per year. Estimate the before-tax RAROC.
RAROC can be used in two different ways. What are they?

title: 13  Financial Innovation and Risk Management Mistakes
text: 13.1 Financial Innovation
crumbs: 13  Financial Innovation and Risk Management Mistakes

title: 13  Financial Innovation and Risk Management Mistakes
text:
Technological changes have led to disintermediation in finance.
New tech-based intermediaries emerge (reintermediation).
Financial firms, like banks, face disruption from FinTech.
Future FinTech will impact digitization, customer behavior, and firm adaptation.
These changes are of particular relevance to risk management professionals.


13.1.1 Technological Advances

Dramatic increase in computer processing power since the 1960s.
Introduction of Robotics Process Automation (RPA) for tasks such as customer account creation and employee hiring.


13.1.1.1 Machine Learning

Differentiates from third industrial revolution through data-driven decision-making.
Applied in areas like credit decision automation, improving upon traditional human-led methods.
Types of Machine Learning:

Unsupervised learning: Identifies patterns and clusters, e.g., customer segmentation.
Supervised learning: Prediction and classification, e.g., house valuation or loan default prediction.
Reinforcement learning: Sequential decision-making, e.g., hedging strategies in derivatives trading.

Challenges:

Overfitting: Model may not generalize to new data.
Solution: Use training, validation, and test datasets to estimate model accuracy.




13.1.1.2 Blockchain Summary

Challenges with Traditional Ledgers: Risks include fraud, lack of trust, hacking, and inconsistencies across multiple record-keepers.
Blockchain as a Solution: A distributed ledger technology allowing multiple entries controlled by a community, aiming to negate the aforementioned risks.
Each block is sealed using a cryptographic hash, ensuring data integrity and tamper-proofing.
In blockchain systems like Bitcoin, miners are rewarded for validating and adding new blocks, thereby ensuring ongoing, decentralized maintenance.
Types of Blockchains:

Permissionless: Open to all, like Bitcoin.
Permissioned: Restricted access; often used by financial institutions for efficient transaction settlements.

Potential in Financial Sector: Blockchain is increasingly being adopted for trading securities, settlements, and back-office functions, aiming for near real-time operations.
Blockchain poses both threats and opportunities—potentially lowering bank fees while opening new service avenues.
May also lower operational risks and regulatory capital requirements.
Adoption and utility in various sectors are still under evaluation; however, it holds significant potential for operational improvements.




13.1.2 Payment Systems

Evolution from cash to digital payments, with rapid adoption in developing countries.
Key attributes: speed, convenience, security, simplicity, and cost.
Innovations: Instant fund transfer (PayPal), digital wallets (Apple Pay), and use of cryptocurrencies.
Security: Focus on biometric authentication to reduce fraud and costs.
Privacy concerns: Data collection and biometric information.
Credit facilities: High-interest credit cards versus FinTech solutions like PayPal’s free 14-day credit.
Future services: Easier foreign exchange and data analytics.
Data-driven credit decisions: Risks around privacy and data selling.
Future trends: Decline of cash, political resistance to full digitization.
Cryptocurrencies: Volatile but secure, growing acceptance.
Central Bank Digital Currencies (CBDCs): Emergence and implications for financial control.



13.1.3 Lending

13.1.3.1 P2P Lending

P2P lending disintermediates traditional banks but adds new intermediaries for risk assessment and transaction facilitation.
P2P often serves borrowers rejected by traditional banks, potentially charging higher interest rates than conventional loans but lower than credit cards.
Diversification and secondary markets are crucial for lenders in P2P platforms to manage risks.



13.1.3.2 Crowdfunding

Crowdfunding democratizes equity investment but faces valuation complexities and regulatory hindrances.
JOBS Act in the U.S. provides some regulatory leeway, but limitations on funding exist.
ICOs represent a speculative, risky, and largely unregulated form of crowdfunding with potential for both fraud and success.




13.1.4 Wealth Management

Traditional Wealth Management:

Highly profitable for banks, with fees ranging from 1% to 1.5% per annum.
Involves client risk assessment and investment recommendations based on risk profiles.

Emergence of index funds in 1975 by John Bogle, offering low fees (0.15%) and no need for human managers.
Robo-Advisers:

Appeared around 2010, subject to regulatory oversight.
Utilize digital platforms for risk assessment and automated portfolio rebalancing.
Lower fees, typically around 0.25% per annum.
Can address cognitive biases.
Popularity may be cyclical, depending on market performance.




13.1.5 InsurTech

Nature of Insurance Industry:

Resistant to disruption due to need for stability and strict regulations.
Difficult for newcomers to enter without significant capital.
Brokers and agents facilitate customer-insurer interactions.
Trend towards disintermediation; websites for direct customer dealings.

Technological Innovations:

Online quote comparison made easy.
Data collection for risk assessment.
Enables precise risk profiling for premium adjustments.
Potentially simplifies claims in case of accidents.

InsurTech unlikely to disrupt, but adoption of technology is advantageous.



13.1.6 Regulation and Compliance

Financial institutions face a high regulatory barrier, affecting small FinTech startups.
Regulators aim to balance innovation with client safety.
“Sandbox” programs provide a testing environment for new financial products.
Challenges include jurisdiction issues and evaluating FinTech’s primarily code-based assets.
Technology simplifies and streamlines regulatory compliance.
Real-time monitoring helps avoid fines and ensures international compliance.



13.1.7 How Should Financial Institutions Respond?

FinTech poses strategic risk to established financial entities.
Institutions should adapt business models to changing consumer behavior.
Various strategies include in-house development, acquisitions, and partnerships with startups.
Flexibility and Adaptability: Key for surviving in the changing landscape.

title: 13  Financial Innovation and Risk Management Mistakes
text:
13.2 Risk Management Mistakes to Avoid

Clear and unambiguous risk limits essential for both financial and non-financial companies.
Limits should be established at the board level and trickled down to individual risk managers.
Daily reports must align with actual gains and losses for accurate risk assessment.
Special Attention to Derivatives:

Close monitoring of derivatives required to differentiate between hedging, speculation, or arbitrage.

Issue of Exceeding Risk Limits for Profit:

Profiting from exceeding risk limits creates a risky culture.
Penalties should apply equally for both profitable and unprofitable violations.

Misconception of Market Predictability:

Success in trading often attributed to luck rather than skill.
Risk limits should not be increased just because a trader shows consistent profits.

Importance of Diversification:

Risks can be halved by diversifying across 20 stocks, making it a poor choice to limit to one market variable.

Necessity of Scenario Analyses and Stress Tests:

Risk measures like VaR should be complemented by stress tests.
Results of stress testing should inform strategic decision-making, often ignored during good times.



13.2.1 Managing the Trading Room

All traders should be scrutinized, even high-performing ones, to ensure they are not taking undue risks or manipulating systems.
Separation of Front, Middle, and Back Office to prevent internal mishandling of trades.
Financial models are not foolproof and should not be blindly trusted.
Model failures have led to significant losses for financial institutions.
Profits from selling exotic instruments should be recognized conservatively over the life of the deal to mitigate the risk of aggressive trading behavior.
Financial institutions should avoid selling inappropriate high-risk products to clients, as it undermines long-term trust and can lead to legal repercussions.
Situations offering seemingly easy profits should be scrutinized for potential hidden risks.



13.2.2 Liquidity Risk

Traders use actively traded instruments to price infrequently traded ones.
Risk arises when assuming these can be traded close to theoretical prices, especially in market shocks.
The need for scenario analyses and stress testing.
Dangers escalate when multiple participants follow the same trading strategy, causing market volatility.
Excessive use of short-term funding for long-term needs increases liquidity risks.
Basel III introduced liquidity requirements.
Lack of transparency in structured products exacerbated the 2007 credit crisis.
Advocates for more information about underlying assets to minimize market disruptions (flight to quality during crisis).



13.2.3 Lessons for Nonfinancial Corporations

13.2.3.1 Understand Your Trades

Corporations should fully understand the trade or trading strategy before execution.
If senior management doesn’t understand a trade, it shouldn’t be approved.
Valuing the financial instrument is a way to ensure understanding.
Relying solely on investment bankers for valuation can be risky.



13.2.3.2 Hedgers vs Speculators

Initially, a trader may focus on hedging but might gradually transition to speculation.
A loss in speculation can lead to risky attempts to recover, potentially causing disaster.
Senior management should set clear risk limits and controls.



13.2.3.3 Treasury as Profit Center: Be Cautious

Making the treasury department a profit center can be tempting but is fraught with risks.
The treasurer might take additional risks to improve the bottom line.
The primary goal of a hedging program is to reduce risks, not to increase profits.
Treating the treasury as a profit center could lead to speculation and financial disasters.




13.2.4 A Final Point

Known Risks: Quantifiable risks like market and credit risks.
Unknown Risks: Known events with indeterminable probability, e.g., operational risks.
Unknowable Risks: Risks where even the event is unknown; often termed as “black swans.”
Known risks are called “risk,” while unknown risks are termed “uncertainty” (Knight, 1921).
Understanding and preparing for various kinds of risks can significantly mitigate the potential for catastrophic losses.


13.2.4.1 Managing Unknown Risks

Subjective probabilities often used.
Flexibility is key; avoid excessive leverage and aim for variable costs.
Insurance products may be developed to cover some unknown risks.



13.2.4.2 Managing Unknowable Risks

Most challenging to handle due to their unpredictability.
Diversification across products and markets can add a layer of protection.
Insurance products for unknowable risks are an area of future development.
